senses.definition: to suppress emotions, desires, or impulses instead of expressing them

examples.x: They are really good at bottling up their true feelings in public.
Don't bottle up your happiness; celebrate your achievements.
He's been bottling up his stress at work for too long.
He tends to bottle up his anger until it becomes overwhelming.

senses.definition: to complete or do something that one could not do earlier, often because of a busy schedule

examples.x: Let's catch up on the gossip over a cup of coffee.
I'll catch up on the reading list during the weekend.
He caught up on exercise after a busy workweek.
After the conference, he caught up on the industry news.

senses.definition: to complete a task or activity thoroughly and entirely

examples.x: After working late into the night, he managed to finish up the report.
Let's finish up the remaining items on the checklist before we leave.
I need to finish up my work before I can join you for lunch.

senses.definition: to stop trying when faced with failures or difficulties

examples.x: He refused to give up even when the odds were stacked against him.
When faced with the challenging puzzle, he was tempted to give up, but he persisted and solved it.
The team was losing, but they didn't give up, and their determination led to a comeback victory.
It’s easy to give up when things get tough, but perseverance is key.
Don’t give up now; you’re almost there.

senses.definition: to stop believing that something is possible or achievable

examples.x: After numerous failed attempts, he decided to give up on his dream of becoming a professional musician.
After facing numerous rejections, she decided to give up on her dream of switching careers and accepted her current job.
It's essential not to give up on your goals, even when faced with obstacles.

senses.definition: to end a phone call by breaking the connection

examples.x: If the call quality is poor, it's better to hang up and try again for a clearer connection.
Don't forget to hang up the phone after our conversation.
He always forgets to hang up the phone, so the battery drains quickly.
It's impolite to hang up on someone without saying goodbye.

senses.definition: to delay the progress of something

examples.x: A technical issue held up the launch of the new website.
She was held up at the office and couldn't make it to the dinner on time.
He apologized for holding up the meeting with his lengthy presentation.
The traffic accident held up the morning commute for hours.

senses.definition: to preserve something at a consistently high standard, price, or level

examples.x: Regular maintenance helps to keep up the performance of the machinery.
She practices her musical skills daily to keep up her performance level.
In order to keep up the quality of their products, the company invests in research and development.
Encouraging messages from loved ones help keep up the soldiers' morale on the battlefield.
The company managed to keep up its commitment to quality despite market fluctuations.

senses.definition: (of a vehicle) to come to a stop

examples.x: The limousine pulled up, and a famous celebrity stepped out.
The delivery van pulled up outside the bakery early in the morning.
Just as I was thinking of leaving, her bike pulled up outside the cafe.

senses.definition: (of a machine or system) to stop working because its parts have become stuck or jammed

examples.x: The machine in the factory seized up, halting production for hours.
The car's brakes seized up, causing a dangerous situation on the road.
The gears in the old clock seized up, and it stopped ticking.
The engine seized up after years of neglect.
The old printer often seizes up when it's trying to print multiple pages at once.

senses.definition: to bring something to a conclusion or resolution, often in a way that was unexpected or unplanned

examples.x: She decided to wind up the meeting early due to lack of progress.
We need to wind up the discussion and make a decision.

senses.definition: to complete a meeting, task, agreement, etc.

examples.x: The committee decided to wrap up the discussion and make a decision.
The teacher will wrap up the class with a brief review of the lesson.
The facilitator will wrap up the training session with a group activity.
The manager suggested wrapping up the negotiation with a final offer.
It's time to wrap up the project and present the final results.

senses.definition: to surrender, typically under pressure or force applied by external factors

examples.x: Facing public backlash, the corporation had to yield up on the controversial product launch.
The environmentalists urged the industrialists to yield up harmful practices for the sake of the planet.
The persistent protests finally led the government to yield up and initiate reforms.
The economic downturn made it difficult for businesses to avoid yielding up some of their profits.
Economic challenges forced the small business owner to yield up control of the company.
